steps.prompt: Two strings crossing in a hoop

graphic.name: crossing-strings
graphic.caption: Stretch two strings across a hoop so they cross. The crossing point P chops each string into two pieces — four pieces in all.

legend.label: first chord: pieces PA and PB
second chord: pieces PC and PD

steps.body:
A chord is a straight line joining two points on a circle — like a string stretched across a hoop.
When two chords cross at a point P inside the circle, each one is cut into two pieces. On the [first chord](blue) the pieces are PA and PB; on the [second chord](orange) they are PC and PD.
This lesson hunts for the rule that ties those four pieces together.

Drag a point to reshape the picture. Two chords always cross at P, splitting into [PA](blue), [PB](blue) on the [first chord](blue) and [PC](orange), [PD](orange) on the [second chord](orange).
Each chord has exactly two pieces. Keep the colours straight: [blue](blue) pieces belong to one chord, [orange](orange) pieces to the other.

steps.prompt: Drag D and watch the two products
steps.subtitle: The four lengths change as you drag, but compare [PA × PB](blue) with [PC × PD](orange) in the readout.
On the first chord, [PA × PB](blue) = 24. On the second, [PC](orange) = 3. Drag to set [PD](orange) so [PC × PD](orange) also makes 24.

feedback.incorrect: You need 3 × PD = 24. Too small and the product is under 24; too big and it's over.

steps.revealText: PD = 8, because 3 × 8 = 24 — exactly matching PA × PB. The two products are always equal.

steps.body:
When two chords cross at P, the two pieces of each chord MULTIPLY to the same value:
[PA](blue) × [PB](blue) = [PC](orange) × [PD](orange).
Here [4 × 6](blue) = 24 and [3 × 8](orange) = 24. It is a product of the pieces, never a sum.

stages: Two chords meet at P, splitting into PA, PB and PC, PD.
Join A to C and D to B. That builds two triangles, PAC and PDB.
The angles at P are vertically opposite, so ∠APC = ∠DPB.
∠A and ∠D open onto the same arc CB, so they are equal — that is last lesson's inscribed-angle idea.
Two equal angles make triangles PAC and PDB similar, so PA / PD = PC / PB. Cross-multiply: PA × PB = PC × PD.

steps.prompt: Find the slip in this working
steps.subtitle: Two chords cross at P with PA = 4, PB = 6, PC = 3. Find PD. One line is wrong — tap it.

lines.text: Crossing chords: PA x PB = PC x PD.
So PC + PD = 24, which gives PD = 24 - 3 = 21.

steps.explanation: The rule multiplies the pieces: PC × PD, not PC + PD. So 3 × PD = 24, which gives PD = 24 ÷ 3 = 8. You divide, not subtract.
steps.hint: Line 1 has a ×, but line 3 switches to +. The two pieces multiply.
steps.doneNote: Exactly — it should be 3 × PD = 24, so PD = 8.

problems.prompt: Two chords cross at P. PA = 4, PB = 6, and PC = 3. Find PD.

feedback.incorrect: The two parts of one chord multiply to the same as the other chord's two parts.

problems.strongHint: PA × PB = PC × PD, so PD = (4 × 6) ÷ 3.

problems.prompt: Two chords cross at P. PA = 6, PB = 6, and PC = 4. Find PD.

feedback.incorrect: The two parts of one chord multiply to the same as the other chord's two parts.

problems.strongHint: PA × PB = PC × PD, so PD = (6 × 6) ÷ 4.

problems.prompt: Two chords cross at P. PA = 3, PB = 8, and PC = 6. Find PD.

feedback.incorrect: The two parts of one chord multiply to the same as the other chord's two parts.

problems.strongHint: PA × PB = PC × PD, so PD = (3 × 8) ÷ 6.

steps.subtitle: Two chords cross at P. [PA](blue) = 5, [PB](blue) = 8, and [PC](orange) = 4. Find [PD](orange).

feedback.incorrect: PA × PB = PC × PD — divide the product by PC.
